skill.name: finishing-a-development-branch
skill.description: Guides an AI coding assistant through the final steps of completing a git feature branch, including tests, merge options, pull request creation, and cleanup.
skill.summary: Complete a git development branch with test checks, merge or pull request options, and controlled cleanup.
skill.author: CodingCossack

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged many shell command patterns, one URL, filesystem redirections, and a weak cryptography blocker. Review found these are mostly documented git, test, and GitHub CLI workflow commands in SKILL.md, not hidden malware or prompt injection. Risk remains medium because the workflow can merge, push, delete branches, and remove git worktrees when used in a real repository.

medium_findings.title: Repository-Mutating Git Workflow
medium_findings.description: The skill instructs the assistant to run git commands that can merge branches, push to origin, force-delete a feature branch after confirmation, and remove a git worktree. These operations match the stated purpose, but they can alter repository state and remove local work if used incorrectly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are explicit and semantically tied to branch completion. The skill includes safeguards, but the repository mutation and deletion impact is real.

low_findings.title: Static External Command Findings Are Mostly Expected
low_findings.description: The many external command detections are visible examples or direct instructions for standard git, test runner, and GitHub CLI actions. No evidence found of obfuscated execution, hidden payloads, or commands unrelated to branch completion.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged commands are readable and aligned with the documented workflow. The file contains no separate executable script or hidden command source.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Is Documentation Reference
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URL points users to GitHub CLI installation documentation when gh is missing. No evidence found that the skill sends repository data or secrets to this URL.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URL is displayed in an echo message and is not used as a network destination by a command. The network activity comes from normal git push and gh PR creation.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Static Finding Appears False Positive
low_findings.description: Static analysis reported a weak cryptographic algorithm at the skill description line. No evidence found of cryptographic code, hashing, encryption, or authentication logic in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Line 3 is natural-language metadata describing a git workflow. The reviewed file does not contain crypto-related implementation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Branch Deletion And Worktree Removal Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The workflow includes git branch deletion and git worktree removal. The discard path requires typed confirmation, but marketplace users should understand these commands can remove local branch and worktree state.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The destructive commands are explicit and include guardrails. The concern is operational safety, not malicious intent.

content.value_statement: Finishing a feature branch can be risky when tests, merge targets, pull requests, and cleanup steps are handled ad hoc. This skill gives Claude, Codex, or Claude Code a structured workflow for safe branch completion.
actual_capabilities: Detects common test commands from project files such as package.json, Cargo.toml, pyproject.toml, go.mod, and Makefile.
Checks likely base branches by comparing merge bases for main, master, and develop.
Presents four completion paths: local merge, pull request, preserve branch, or discard branch.
Runs tests before proceeding and after a successful local merge.
Creates a pull request through the GitHub CLI when gh is installed and authenticated.
Cleans up a git worktree after merge or confirmed discard paths.
limitations: It assumes the repository uses git and has command line tools available.
Automatic base branch detection only considers main, master, and develop.
Pull request creation requires GitHub CLI installation and authentication.
It does not auto-resolve merge conflicts or failing tests.

use_cases.title: Solo Developer Finishing A Feature
use_cases.description: Run tests, choose a merge or pull request path, and avoid forgetting cleanup steps.

use_cases.title: Team Contributor Preparing Review
use_cases.description: Push a branch and create a pull request with a structured summary and test plan.

use_cases.title: Maintainer Cleaning Completed Worktrees
use_cases.description: Merge or discard completed work while preserving explicit confirmation before destructive cleanup.

prompt_templates.title: Finish Current Branch
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request after implementation is complete.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the finishing-a-development-branch workflow on my current repository. Verify tests first and ask me which completion option to use.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare A Pull Request
prompt_templates.scenario: Create a reviewable branch without merging locally.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the branch finishing workflow and guide me through creating a pull request. Keep the worktree intact after the PR is created.

prompt_templates.title: Merge Locally After Tests
prompt_templates.scenario: Complete a branch directly into its base branch.
prompt_templates.prompt: Finish this development branch by identifying the base branch, running tests, merging locally if I approve, rerunning tests, and cleaning up afterward.

prompt_templates.title: Audit Before Discarding Work
prompt_templates.scenario: Advanced cleanup when a branch may no longer be needed.
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the discard path from the branch finishing workflow. Show me the branch, commits, and worktree path before asking for the exact discard confirmation.

output_examples.input: Finish this feature branch and create a pull request.
output_examples.output: The assistant verifies tests, identifies the likely base branch, confirms the pull request option, pushes the feature branch, creates the PR, and reports the PR URL.

output_examples.input: Merge this branch back into main if it is ready.
output_examples.output: The assistant runs the project tests, checks the base branch, asks for the local merge option, merges into the base branch, reruns tests, and reports the merge result.

output_examples.input: This branch is obsolete. Help me remove it safely.
output_examples.output: The assistant lists the branch, commits, and worktree path, then waits for the required typed confirmation before deleting the branch and cleaning the worktree.

best_practices: Start from a clean working tree so test and merge results are easy to interpret.
Review the detected base branch before approving merge or pull request creation.
Use the discard option only after checking the listed commits and worktree path.
anti_patterns: Do not use this workflow to bypass failing tests or unresolved merge conflicts.
Do not choose discard unless the branch commits are no longer needed.
Do not create a pull request before confirming gh is installed and authenticated.

faq.question: Does this skill write application code?
faq.answer: No. It manages the final git workflow after implementation work is complete.

faq.question: Can it create a pull request?
faq.answer: Yes. It can use the GitHub CLI to push the branch and create a pull request.

faq.question: Does it delete branches automatically?
faq.answer: It deletes branches only in the merge cleanup path or after explicit typed confirmation for discard.

faq.question: What happens if tests fail?
faq.answer: The workflow stops and reports a blocked test state before merge, pull request, or cleanup steps continue.

faq.question: What base branches does it detect?
faq.answer: It checks main, master, and develop, then asks the user when the result is ambiguous.

faq.question: Is this safe for worktrees?
faq.answer: It includes worktree cleanup for merge and discard paths, so users should confirm the target path before approving destructive actions.
